briefing.headline: 11 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: EMJ, EMJ, SJTREM. Main themes: guideline or formal recommendation, reviews, evidence synthesis. Best early reads: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches; Clinical decision rules for obtaining chest radiography in adult patients presenting to the emergency department with non-traumatic chest pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
briefing.editorialHeadline: From Hyperkalemia Protocols to Community Care: Navigating Today's ED Challenges
briefing.editorialSummary:
The spectrum of today’s readings demands vigilance, whether managing acute electrolyte crises or refining prehospital algorithms. For instance, the management of acute hyperkalemia requires a sharp focus: while calcium remains paramount if ECG changes or K+ $\geq 6.5 \text{ mmol/L}$ are present, the utility of inhaled beta-agonists alongside insulin-glucose for shifting potassium is a key adjunct to remember, all while preemptively guarding against subsequent hypoglycemia.

In the realm of undifferentiated illness, the guidance for suspected poisoning emphasizes adopting a systematic, toxidromic mindset—using general frameworks to scaffold care until specific poison center protocols can be engaged. Similarly, when assessing non-traumatic chest pain, the evidence base for routine CXR decision rules remains heterogeneous, suggesting caution against over-reliance on any single algorithm.

Beyond the ED doors, the potential for senior-level care to extend into the community, particularly for complex patients like those on anticoagulation with head injuries, is a growing operational frontier. These varied threads—from optimizing resuscitation sequences in pediatric cardiac arrest to refining sepsis prediction using prehospital data—underscore that today’s practice requires integrating the latest evidence into established, yet adaptable, clinical workflows.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing acute hyperkalemia, prioritize calcium if ECG changes or K+ $\geq 6.5 \text{ mmol/L}$ are present. Use insulin-glucose with inhaled beta-agonists for potassium shifting, but always anticipate and preemptively treat for hypoglycemia. Remember that newer binders are promising but lack acute validation, and do not rely on sodium polystyrene sulfonate.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review synthesizes current evidence for managing acute, potentially life-threatening hyperkalemia in the ED, focusing on practical application and areas of clinical uncertainty.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review synthesizes current evidence for managing acute, potentially life-threatening hyperkalemia in the ED, focusing on practical application and areas of clinical uncertainty. The core principles emphasize immediate stabilization, particularly with IV calcium salts if ECG changes are present or potassium is $\geq 6.5 \text{ mmol/L}$. While insulin-glucose remains a mainstay for shifting potassium, the risk of subsequent hypoglycemia must be actively managed, especially in non-diabetics. The authors highlight the utility of inhaled beta-agonists like salbutamol as a synergistic adjunct to insulin. Furthermore, they caution against outdated practices, noting that sodium polystyrene sulfonate is no longer recommended, and that the role of sodium bicarbonate is limited to specific metabolic acidosis contexts. For refractory cases, dialysis remains the definitive intervention.

dailyEditorial.title: Clinical decision rules for obtaining chest radiography in adult patients presenting to the emergency department with non-traumatic chest pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing non-traumatic chest pain, don't rely on these existing CDRs to definitively guide CXR ordering, as the evidence base is weak and heterogeneous. If you must use a rule, be aware that the comparative data suggests potential trade-offs between sensitivity and specificity that might not translate to real-world practice. Remember that the authors themselves cautioned that prospective derivation using rigorous standards is needed before adopting any single algorithm.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackled the resource drain caused by routine chest radiography in non-traumatic chest pain, noting that imaging is used frequently despite yielding actionable findings in only a…
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackled the resource drain caused by routine chest radiography in non-traumatic chest pain, noting that imaging is used frequently despite yielding actionable findings in only a small minority of cases. The authors pooled data from several existing clinical decision rules (CDRs), specifically comparing the Hess and Rothrock algorithms. While the meta-analysis provided quantitative comparisons, the overall findings were somewhat mixed, showing that the Hess rule was more sensitive but might not significantly reduce imaging rates, whereas the Rothrock rule showed a positive likelihood ratio compared to Hess. Crucially, the authors concluded that due to the limited number of studies, high heterogeneity, and the retrospective nature of the cohorts, neither established rule is definitively recommendable at this time.

dailyEditorial.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For any patient with pTBI who is considered a potential survivor, ensure aggressive management protocols are followed, emphasizing early CTA and adherence to established TBI guidelines. Be mindful that local compliance with prophylactic antibiotics and timely ICP monitoring may be suboptimal, so actively audit these elements at the bedside. Remember that while outcomes can be good, adherence to established protocols is key to optimizing care.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This report details the management of civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury (pTBI) encountered in South-East Norway.
dailyEditorial.summary: This report details the management of civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury (pTBI) encountered in South-East Norway. While the authors note that most survivors of this injury achieve a favorable outcome, their review highlighted significant gaps in local guideline adherence. Specifically, compliance rates for prophylactic antibiotics, antimicrobials, intracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring, and timely surgical revision within the first 12 hours were lower than anticipated. The overall message is that despite the high mortality associated with pTBI, aggressive management following established TBI principles remains crucial for potential survivors.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When faced with an undiagnosed toxic exposure, adopt a systematic, toxidromic mindset focusing on anticipated physiological changes rather than waiting for a definitive diagnosis. Use this guideline for initial triage and management scaffolding, but always pivot to the specific, established protocols from your local poison center or TOXBASE for definitive antidotal therapy or decontamination decisions. Remember this is a general framework, not a replacement for specific agent management.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service addresses the notoriously difficult management of patients where poisoning is suspected but the agent re…
dailyEditorial.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service addresses the notoriously difficult management of patients where poisoning is suspected but the agent remains unidentified. The authors have developed a generalized clinical approach designed to guide initial assessment and support the identification of potential toxins within the chaotic ED setting. A key feature of this guidance is its adoption of a toxidromic approach, meaning management heavily emphasizes understanding the toxicokinetics of potential poisons and anticipating how the patient's clinical status might evolve. It is crucial to note that this document is intended as a broad framework and explicitly does not supersede the detailed, poison-specific advice available from resources like TOXBASE or local poison centers.

dailyEditorial.title: Sequence of advanced airway management and epinephrine administration for paediatric patients with non-shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For pediatric patients in non-shockable OHCA, the sequence of initiating advanced airway management versus epinephrine administration does not appear to influence 1-month survival or favorable neurological outcomes based on this data. This suggests that in the field, the priority should remain on rapid, simultaneous implementation of both interventions rather than adhering to a strict sequence. However, remember this is observational data from a specific registry, so clinical judgment must guide the decision-making process.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis examined the optimal timing of advanced airway management (AAM) versus epinephrine administration in pediatric patients presenting with non-shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective analysis examined the optimal timing of advanced airway management (AAM) versus epinephrine administration in pediatric patients presenting with non-shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). Using data from a large Japanese registry spanning 2014 to 2022, the authors compared outcomes between groups where AAM was performed first versus those where epinephrine was given first. After propensity score matching, the study found no statistically significant difference in 1-month survival, favorable neurological outcome, or return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) when comparing the two sequences. Overall, the findings suggest that the order in which these two critical interventions are initiated may not impact immediate post-resuscitation outcomes in this specific pediatric population.

dailyEditorial.title: Community emergency medicine: a service review of patients that sustain head injuries in the community--can early intervention by a senior clinical decision maker provide care closer to home?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing community patients with head injuries, remember that senior physician input can be safely delivered outside the ED, especially for those on anticoagulation, provided local guidelines are updated and followed. The core principle is identifying patients who need more than paramedic scope but are stable enough for home management. Always confirm local protocols for self-tasking and escalation pathways to ensure appropriate risk stratification.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review details the operational model of a Physician Response Unit (PRU) in Southeast Wales, which deploys a physician-paramedic team to provide senior emergency medicine decision-making directly within the communit…
dailyEditorial.summary: This review details the operational model of a Physician Response Unit (PRU) in Southeast Wales, which deploys a physician-paramedic team to provide senior emergency medicine decision-making directly within the community setting. The unit actively manages patients, including those with head injuries, who might otherwise require an Emergency Department visit. A key aspect highlighted is the empowerment of Emergency Medicine Physicians (EMPs) to manage complex cases, such as anticoagulated patients, at home, aligning with updated NICE guidelines. The service operates by either self-tasking based on call handler data or responding to ambulance requests, aiming to safely divert appropriate care away from the ED. This model demonstrates a structured approach to extending acute, senior-level care into the community.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: While prehospital scores like qSOFA show utility in predicting septic shock, remember that no single tool predicts all aspects of sepsis severity or mortality. Use these scores as adjuncts to clinical judgment, not as standalone determinants for escalating care. Be mindful that the predictive value of tools like NEWS2 may vary by sex, suggesting a need for tailored interpretation at the bedside.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prospective cohort study investigated whether variables captured prehospital by EMS personnel could predict the subsequent clinical course in patients presenting with suspected sepsis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This prospective cohort study investigated whether variables captured prehospital by EMS personnel could predict the subsequent clinical course in patients presenting with suspected sepsis. The authors analyzed data from 398 patients, correlating prehospital findings—including oxygen saturation, systolic blood pressure, and qSOFA—with outcomes like SOFA score, development of septic shock, and 30-day mortality. While several prehospital factors were associated with specific outcomes, the analysis noted that no single variable predicted all three endpoints. Notably, the study found that qSOFA had a higher AUC for predicting septic shock compared to NEWS2, and that NEWS2 performed better for predicting shock in males versus females. The overall conclusion advises caution regarding the use of these prehospital tools in isolation.

dailyEditorial.title: EMCrit 424 – 2026 Crashing PE Update
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing a critically ill patient with PE, use the integrated risk stratification framework to guide therapy rather than relying solely on the AHA's categorical guidelines. Remember that the decision tree must weigh the need for reperfusion (thrombolysis/thrombectomy) against the patient's hemodynamic stability and bleeding risk. Be mindful that this synthesis is designed to streamline complex decision-making at the bedside.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence for managing hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism, effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratification framework.
dailyEditorial.summary: This update synthesizes the latest evidence for managing hemodynamically significant pulmonary embolism, effectively merging the new AHA consensus guidelines with the established EMCrit risk-stratification framework. It provides a comprehensive, algorithm-driven approach to treating sick PE, covering the spectrum of interventions from systemic thrombolysis to mechanical thrombectomy and ECMO support. The goal is to create a cohesive, practical algorithm that helps clinicians reconcile the sometimes disparate language of major guidelines with actionable bedside management. The integration of recent randomized data strengthens the existing EMCrit recommendations, offering a more unified pathway for complex cases.

dailyEditorial.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing suspected AIS-LVO, the evidence synthesis suggests a strong preference for direct transport to a comprehensive center (mothership) when feasible, as this may expedite definitive care. However, the authors note that the current literature presents conflicting data, so local protocols must remain adaptable. Always consider the feasibility of rapid transfer versus the potential delay inherent in a local workup.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the ongoing debate regarding the optimal pre-hospital management pathway for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke due to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO).
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the ongoing debate regarding the optimal pre-hospital management pathway for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke due to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO). Specifically, it compares the outcomes of the 'mothership' approach—direct transport to a comprehensive stroke center—against the 'drip-and-ship' model, which involves initial evaluation at a local facility before transfer. The authors synthesized evidence to determine if one strategy offers a superior clinical benefit in terms of patient outcomes. Given the high stakes in acute stroke care, clarifying this logistical pathway is crucial for improving timely reperfusion therapy.

dailyEditorial.title: Impact of aspirin use on the modified brain injury guidelines for the management of mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When risk-stratifying patients with mild tICH, consider the impact of pre-existing aspirin use. The current data suggest that aspirin users may be safely managed with observation rather than mandatory transfer to a regional trauma center, potentially streamlining disposition. Remember that this is a proposed expansion of guidelines, and definitive management decisions should await confirmation from larger, prospective studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This article addresses the role of pre-injury aspirin use in the management of isolated mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (tICH) within the framework of the modified Brain Injury Guidelines (mBIG1).
dailyEditorial.summary: This article addresses the role of pre-injury aspirin use in the management of isolated mild traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (tICH) within the framework of the modified Brain Injury Guidelines (mBIG1). The authors specifically investigated whether aspirin use elevates the risk of deterioration in this patient subset. Their conclusion is that the mBIG1 criteria may be safely expanded to encompass patients who are on aspirin prior to injury. They suggest that these individuals might be managed effectively with observation at a lower level of care, potentially negating the need for transfer to a regional trauma center. However, the authors caution that this recommendation is preliminary and requires confirmation through further prospective clinical studies.

dailyEditorial.title: A randomised controlled trial of defibrillation with manual pressure augmentation during out-of-hospital cardiac arrest
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For initial management of shockable OHCA, current evidence does not support incorporating manual pressure augmentation into the defibrillation sequence, despite its ability to lower transthoracic impedance. Continue standard high-quality CPR and defibrillation protocols without adding this step unless local protocols mandate it. Remember that impedance changes alone do not equate to improved patient survival.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of adding manual pressure augmentation (MPA) during defibrillation for patients presenting with initially shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA).
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of adding manual pressure augmentation (MPA) during defibrillation for patients presenting with initially shockable out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The primary objective was to determine if this adjunct intervention improves survival or any other relevant clinical endpoint compared to standard defibrillation protocols. While the study did report a measurable reduction in transthoracic impedance when MPA was applied, the overall conclusion was that this intervention did not translate into improved survival rates or any other measured clinical outcomes in this population.

dailyEditorial.title: Impact of Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Health Care Workers' Physical Health Parameters on Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation Performance Quality in Simulated Manikin-Based Settings-A Systematic Review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Remember that CPR quality hinges significantly on the rescuer's physical reserves; adequate depth and sustained rate require more than just knowledge. While we can't screen every provider for peak physical fitness at the bedside, recognizing fatigue or underlying deconditioning as a potential limiter to performance is key. Be mindful that this review is based on simulation, so interpret the findings as emphasizing the importance of provider wellness alongside protocol adherence.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes evidence regarding how the physical health and fitness levels of healthcare workers directly influence the quality of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) performance when simulated.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes evidence regarding how the physical health and fitness levels of healthcare workers directly influence the quality of cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) performance when simulated. The authors conclude that an individual's underlying physical capacity is a critical determinant in maintaining high-quality CPR efforts. Specifically, sustaining the necessary depth and appropriate frequency of chest compressions demands both muscular strength and cardiovascular endurance from the providers. This suggests that CPR performance isn't solely dependent on training protocols but is also intrinsically linked to the physical state of the rescuer. While the findings are based on simulated manikin models, the implication for real-world performance is noteworthy.

dailyEditorial.title: Helicopter Emergency Medical Services attendance is associated with favourable survival outcomes in major trauma: derivation and internal validation of prediction models in a regional trauma system
dailyEditorial.takeaway: The data suggest that HEMS transport provides a measurable survival advantage in major trauma, especially for the critically injured where the benefit is greatest. While the overall benefit is clear, remember that the strongest signal was seen in the moderate probability of survival group. Clinicians should recognize that advanced pre-hospital care, not just transport, appears to be key for improving outcomes in the most unstable patients.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This retrospective analysis looked at a large cohort of major trauma patients attended by Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) in a regional system over nearly a decade.
dailyEditorial.summary: This retrospective analysis looked at a large cohort of major trauma patients attended by Helicopter Emergency Medical Services (HEMS) in a regional system over nearly a decade. The core finding is that HEMS attendance is associated with better survival outcomes compared to what would be expected based on injury severity alone. Specifically, the observed survival rates exceeded the expected rates, suggesting a genuine benefit. This benefit was particularly pronounced in severely injured patients who were in a moderate probability of survival bracket. Furthermore, the authors noted that pre-hospital advanced interventions, like those provided by PHEA, appeared to confer a survival benefit even in patients with a low predicted chance of survival.

dailyEditorial.title: A systematic review of mortality and severe morbidities following newborn chest compressions at birth
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the high heterogeneity across existing studies, do not interpret variations in reported outcomes as evidence of systematic improvements or declines in care quality. When assessing the utility of CC, remember that current data lacks standardization across key variables like gestational age and resuscitation timing. Future practice should await larger, prospective studies adhering to strict protocols to guide definitive recommendations.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesized data on mortality and severe morbidities following the use of chest compressions (CC) at birth in neonates.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesized data on mortality and severe morbidities following the use of chest compressions (CC) at birth in neonates. The authors conclude that the wide variability observed in outcome estimates across different studies cannot be reliably attributed to simple factors like changes in time or location. They highlight significant gaps in the current literature, specifically noting the difficulty in accurately differentiating deaths due to underlying disease versus those directly attributable to the resuscitation process. Furthermore, the need for better stratification based on gestational age, the setting of birth, the level of care provided, and the timing of CC remains apparent. Ultimately, the review points toward a critical need for prospective studies designed according to a standardized protocol, such as the Neonatal Utstein guidelines, to improve the rigor and comparability of future evidence.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular resuscitation: an expert practice review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering EVR for refractory shock or arrest, remember that these techniques are designed to bridge care, not replace definitive management. The review details escalating interventions, so approach vascular access and initial balloon occlusion with a clear understanding of the underlying physiology. Be mindful that while adoption is growing, the utility and implementation of these advanced methods still require careful consideration of local protocols and institutional expertise.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This expert review synthesizes the rapidly evolving field of endovascular resuscitation (EVR), which encompasses novel therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest toward definitive ca…
dailyEditorial.summary: This expert review synthesizes the rapidly evolving field of endovascular resuscitation (EVR), which encompasses novel therapies designed to bridge critically ill patients in shock or cardiac arrest toward definitive care. The authors structure the discussion around practical case scenarios, specifically addressing out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and non-compressible hemorrhage. They provide a comprehensive overview of the underlying physiology and techniques for three escalating interventions: resuscitative endovascular balloon occlusion of the aorta, selective aortic arch perfusion, and extracorporeal cardiopulmonary resuscitation. Given the growing adoption of these techniques, the review serves as a crucial guide for emergency physicians to understand the rationale, efficacy, and safety profiles of EVR modalities.

dailyEditorial.title: On the optimal sodium correction rate in hyponatraemia and clinical outcome: a meta-analysis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Given the meta-analysis results, a more aggressive approach to sodium correction rates in hyponatremia appears associated with better survival, even when looking at thresholds of 8, 10, or 12 mmol/L/day. However, remember that this is an association, not proof of causation, and the risk of ODS remains a critical consideration that must guide your rate decisions. Proceed cautiously, balancing the mortality benefit against the risk of demyelination.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This meta-analysis synthesized data from 11 retrospective studies to evaluate the impact of different sodium correction rates on mortality and osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) in patients with hyponatremia.
dailyEditorial.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized data from 11 retrospective studies to evaluate the impact of different sodium correction rates on mortality and osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) in patients with hyponatremia. The authors specifically analyzed three thresholds for defining rapid versus slow correction: 8, 10, and 12 mmol/L per 24 hours. The overall finding suggests that rapid sodium correction, regardless of the specific threshold used, was associated with improved survival outcomes. While the data is compelling regarding survival benefits, the authors caution that this does not establish a definitive causal link, and they reiterate the persistent risk of ODS.

dailyEditorial.title: Robotic surgery in colorectal emergencies: a systematic review of current evidence
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When considering robotic assistance for acute colorectal emergencies, remember that technical feasibility does not equate to immediate optimal performance. Focus on ensuring your team has undergone specific training for the emergency context, and advocate for developing dedicated institutional pathways rather than relying on ad-hoc use. Proceed with caution, recognizing that institutional readiness is the biggest predictor of success.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of robotic platforms in the management of colorectal emergencies.
dailyEditorial.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the current evidence regarding the use of robotic platforms in the management of colorectal emergencies. The authors conclude that while robotic assistance is indeed feasible and safe for these acute settings, its successful implementation is not automatic. Optimal outcomes appear contingent upon more than just the technology itself; the literature strongly suggests a need for structured, additional training for the surgical team and the establishment of dedicated institutional protocols. Essentially, the technology is ready, but the human infrastructure supporting it needs to mature to maximize benefits and minimize risks in the acute care environment.

dailyEditorial.title: New Guidelines Emphasize Outpatient Treatment of Asymptomatic Hypertension
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When managing asymptomatic hypertension in the ED, use these updated guidelines to solidify your decision to discharge versus admit. Focus on ensuring the patient understands the necessity of follow-up with their primary care provider to initiate appropriate outpatient therapy. Remember that the goal is risk stratification, not acute management, so discharge counseling is key.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The latest updates from ACC/AHA and the 2025 ACEP guidelines are refining the approach to managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department.
dailyEditorial.summary: The latest updates from ACC/AHA and the 2025 ACEP guidelines are refining the approach to managing asymptomatic hypertension encountered in the emergency department. The core message seems to be a stronger emphasis on directing appropriate patients to outpatient follow-up rather than initiating immediate inpatient management or medication changes in the ED setting. These guidelines provide clearer algorithms for determining when a patient presenting with elevated blood pressure but no acute symptoms truly requires immediate escalation of care versus when routine primary care follow-up is sufficient. This shift helps streamline ED resources by better defining the threshold for initiating pharmacologic management.

dailyEditorial.title: Comparison of three point-of-care ultrasound techniques to confirm endotracheal tube placement: A randomized clinical trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When confirming ETT placement post-RSI, remember that multiple ultrasound approaches show high diagnostic yield. If speed is a major concern, TUS appears to be the fastest method reported, though all techniques are reliable. Don't rely on any single method, and always correlate ultrasound findings with standard clinical assessment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized clinical trial compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation (RSI).
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized clinical trial compared three different point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) techniques for confirming endotracheal tube (ETT) placement following rapid sequence intubation (RSI). The study found that all three assessed ultrasound methods demonstrated high diagnostic accuracy in confirming the tube's location. Notably, the technique utilizing transcranial ultrasound (TUS) was reported to provide the quickest confirmation time among the methods tested. This suggests that while multiple modalities are effective, there may be a practical advantage in speed when selecting a confirmation technique in the acute setting.

dailyEditorial.title: Triage administration of sucrose for gastroenteritis in children; a randomized controlled trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not routinely administer sucrose solution to children with gastroenteritis in the ED, as this single dose did not improve oral rehydration intake. Focus management on standard supportive care and appropriate electrolyte replacement guidelines. Be mindful that this trial only assessed the effect of a single dose.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial investigated the utility of administering sucrose solution to children presenting to the emergency department with gastroenteritis.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated the utility of administering sucrose solution to children presenting to the emergency department with gastroenteritis. The primary endpoint assessed was the improvement in oral rehydration intake following a single dose of sucrose. The authors concluded that providing a single dose of sucrose solution does not enhance the amount of oral rehydration fluid consumed by these pediatric patients in the ED setting. This finding directly challenges the routine practice of prophylactic sucrose administration in this population.
